speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, we left off as you went to this army camp to work. Did the sergeant ever use that whip that you described on anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never again. Never again. He was, like they say in English, our savior. I couldn't believe that something unusual, that somebody upstairs, somebody from nowhere, came to me and now trying to help me. I didn't believe that there's such an animal in this world to help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What did I do? But fortunately, there is somebody. There's always-- that's why I believe in miracles. This guy was a miracle. How did the rest of the soldiers in this army camp behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were all nice now. They were very nice now, because it was a regular army camp, you know. Nobody hurt us, because since he was in charge, you know. Of course, he was afraid-- they were afraid. They knew-- maybe they were-- they-- they-- they were-- they-- he was an-- they were-- they were actually-- maybe they were obeying his order or something. I don't know. But I never asked questions, you know. And they were nice to me. They were nice, yeah. They were nice to the 26 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular dress, you know. Regular clothes, you know. Civilian clothes, you know. Shoes, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: From home?

speaker: interviewee
text: From home. The way I was [INAUDIBLE], you know. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these people in the camp, the soldiers, did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They knew we were Jewish, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, we were a few weeks, you know. At night we used to go home, you know. He used to take us home, because he didn't leave us to open the gate. He brought in the truck inside. He used to give us food, you know. The soldiers used to give us food, you know. The mess hall used to give us the leftovers, you know. And so we-- after we-- so when he drove the truck in, he didn't leave us right in the gate, you know. If he would have left us near the gate, you know, they would have searched us. They would take everything away. So he knew that, so he drove it in the ghetto, and he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he left us off. And we were happy, because I was able to bring some food for my family-- my grand-- my stepmother, my father, my younger brothers, you know. It was almost like coming-- they looked forward to when I came home. They were all like-- they were like little puppies from looking forward for their-- for the-- the the dog to come home, the mother dog to come home. It was like-- I feel like I'm helping my [INAUDIBLE] family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this condition last?

speaker: interviewee
text: It lasted-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it lasted till, um, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a few months it lasted. But I-- I want to stress something else now. I didn't know what to do for this fellow. I wanted to do something for this sergeant. I compensate him for his goodness, and I didn't know what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one time I talked to him. I said, do you have kids? He says, yes, I got a daughter. She's going to be 13 years old. What's her name? He told me her name. He told me. I wrote it down. Very nicely I wrote it down. I made the initials. I made, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same house we lived in in Poland, downstairs there was a jeweler. He had the equipment. So I took a spoon, a silver spoon, that-- we had a silver spoon. When I was born, I think my grandmother gave us a silver spoon. It was like for the oldest grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: I took that silver spoon, I it melt-- this man had it melted. And he made a heart out of it, you know? And I-- this man engraved her name. And he had it polished, you know. And I gave it to him. Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was most gracious thing. He didn't know what to do for me. He was laughing. He says, my, god, you feel-- you sure-- he say, you sure were calm. You told-- you asked me all those questions, you know, but you had a reason why you asked me those questions, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you told me that he did other favors for you. What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The favors were he gave us food. He gave me some shoes sometimes, you know. Same camp, you know, then they brought in the Russian POWs, they brought in. There were some Jewish people in the Russian POWs, you know, that nobody knew about it. He knew about it. I remember one Jewish man from-- from Moscow or somewhere. Moscow or Minsk, you know. He was caught in the front line. His name was Yosef Minske or something. And I remember he was a-- he was a-- a-- a carpenter. And he knew he was Jewish. I knew he was Jewish, you know. Because there was a law that if he was Jewish, he had to be gone back to the SS, you know, as a POW, as a Prisoner of War. And he-- and-- and-- and he couldn't stay with us, you know, in this particular camp, you know. But this man, his sergeant, never said a word. And he was there for a long time. When I left this camp, he was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yosef.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the sergeant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schrader. Schrader. Sergeant Schrader. I wish I knew his name. After the war, I wish I could have found him, but I didn't know. I remember him [INAUDIBLE] very tall. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did he stay with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one time I went over to him. I says, Sergeant Schrader, my father is one of the best tailors in-- in Radom. He's a woman's tailor. He took up tailoring in-- I mean, he learned tailoring in Buenos Aires, you know. He was in Argentina. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he came back from Argentina, you know, and he knew all these fashions. He knew design. He knows how to make-- tell me, perhaps you have a place for him, too. Please, help him. He says, I'll see what I can do about it. That's it. And he never said not a word. He never said to me, I'm going to pick him up, you know. I'll take him or bring him. Never said nothing. Then-- then about-- this was going on about-- when I was there, it must have been about, oh, a month later, the-- they called the [INAUDIBLE]. The [INAUDIBLE] means, you know, they took out the Jews, you know, on the street in 1942, mid '42. I can't remember the month exactly. I think it was in June or July. When the Germans-- about 5 o'clock in the morning, you know-- knocked on our door-- it was loud. It was awful. Rouse, rouse. And, oh, there was all noise. We saw blood. We had a-- like a rail coming down the steps, you know. We lived on the second floor. And we heard, rouse, rouse. And there were-- whoever went slow was beaten with the butt from the rifle. And if anyone was pregnant, they shot. We saw-- it's the first time I saw people actually being physically shot. I never saw it in my life that. And I saw-- they threw kids out from the windows. They shot them in the air like a practice shooting. I saw-- and they-- we all went out on the street. We stayed from 4:30 in the morning. Our street was a very huge street, because on Thursdays we used to have, like, a flea market, in that street, you know. So it was very large. Very, very wide. And there was-- they had all the Jews, you know, who were in the ghetto was in that street, in that particular-- but when it got lighter, we heard-- that's all we heard is shouting, shooting, you know. Screaming, hollering, whipping, beating, people falling down. The-- I remembered the chief rabbi was shot then. The chief cantor was shot there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- that's-- I think it's Keslenberg or somebody. No, I can't remember. You know, it's been a long time, you know. And I remember these details. I remember and-- and the-- I know the Rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. They used to call him [INAUDIBLE]. He lived on [INAUDIBLE], which means the new-- the new world, you know. He was a very pious person. When people were sick, they used to go to him for help. He was very, very most-- I used to-- when I used to go by from school-- you know, I was a young kid and all-- and I used to go by and near his house, you know, I felt I'm going through, like, a god. I felt very, very emotional when I used to go by there. I felt this is-- this must be something. God is there. Because he knew how to help people. He used to-- knew how to-- how to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was give-- like, he knew how to help the sickness-- the sick people. If they had the misfortune, you know. So I felt very important by going by his house, Even. though I never saw him in person. And, yeah, I did-- my grandfather one time took me in there when my mother passed away. He took me in there one time, and he blessed me. I forget that. He was shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all saw it. Not only me. Everybody saw it. The whole city saw it. There was people laying there like-- like-- like flies on the street. Anybody who was pregnant and people-- old women, or anybody's an invalid, anybody couldn't walk, you know. I remember something else now. The building, on the top of the building in our-- on this attic, we always heard there was an-- a child, a mongrel child, you know. We never knew who it belonged to, but we heard that a young kid, you know-- don't go to the attic, because there was a child-- a parent-- a mother put a child up there, because the child was mongrel and she was ashamed, you know. Something, you know. And I saw the child being brought down and shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took all the hospital people who lived in the hospital-- we had a Jewish hospital-- every-- all the people, the patients, were shot on the spot. They took out the people from the crazy house, because we had a crazy house there, all shot. They took out the people from the jails.

speaker: interviewer
text: First thing, they asked anybody, are you intelligent? They took out anybody with intelligence, they shot-- he was shot. Then they took out the people who are pious, like rabbis, put them aside, shot. And then something unusual happened. Then Schrader came, Schrader. He walked in there. He was very upset. And he asked the SS, where is my Jews? I need them. They're doing a very important job for the government. Where are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS-- they didn't know that he was conning them. He-- they said, go ahead and find your Jews. He went around. He says-- I can't remember. He called my name [INAUDIBLE]. I think he called me Munnsfeld or something, you know. Come out. My-- I need my Jews, you know. I need my Juden. My [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They work for me. I need them very bad. I cannot exist without them. And he came to me. I stayed in the line with my brother, my younger brother, my stepmother, and my-- and my father. And he came in, he grabbed me by my-- my neck. Come out. He is try-- he was trying to impress the Germans that he was a very mean man. And there's your goddamn father, the tailor. Come out, too. And he pulled him out, too. Now we were 27 Jews. We all were in the truck. And we went out. The rest went away to the train, and I never saw my younger brother again, or my mother-- my stepmother. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And what happened to the older brother? Well, he escaped-- he was taken away, and he escaped from the train. About three or four hours later, five hours later, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he came to the gate of the base. Before we went home at night, we went home to the camp. This time, the sergeant said, oh, you-- from now on, you're going to stay here. You're going to stay. I'll make some room in the barracks, you know, someplace like-- you're going to stay right here. So we was 27 Jews, with my father. Later on, my brother came. He was with my-- he was very, very disturbed, an very emotionally crying, you know. I need my brother, you know. I need my father, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the sergeant-- they-- not the sergeant-- the guard near the-- the sentry man before they go in the base, asked him, who is your father? And who is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't know my father, because my father was a new man there. But they knew me. The flower fellow. Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah. They called me. I called Schrader. He says, OK, come inside now. And I-- and now we were 28. He took him in. And every other time the Germans came, the Nazis came, they wanted to take us out that particular time, they came there with trucks one time. They wanted-- they went in with orders from the kommandantur to get those 28 Jews. They were-- we were never released. He was finding any kind of way to keep us. And my father became a tailor for the-- the headquarters. He was fixing for a general, one time. For a colonel. He was fixing their suits, you know. They were fixing their-- their-- their-- their pants, you know. And it was called the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He worked with it. He became the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] tailor. And they never know he was Jewish, not Jewish. They know one thing-- he was a good tailor. They said [INAUDIBLE] good [INAUDIBLE]. And this was going on until it exhausted. Until the SS came. And there was no other ammunition for him to say-- he lost his power, the sergeant, you know. And they took us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother tell you how he escaped from the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never asked him. He just escaped. My brother was a very, very-- my brother, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] is a very smart fellow. He's got-- he always had-- before we did anything, he has to think. He has very, very good ways about himself, you know. I always admired him. Because he had the right decisions. He used to help us, you know. He used to worry about us, you know. I used to worry about him. We were very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when he escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '42-- this was happening in '42-- so I was-- '42, I was 17. He was 15 years old. But he was very small.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Nazis came into this camp, what happened to the 26 or 28 Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us into a small ghetto. The remaining-- they made-- from the big ghetto who went away to Majdanek or to Treblinka or someplace else, you know, they formed-- a small ghetto is formed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this ghetto was located in Radom. It consisted of about three or four streets. It was called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Street. They put us up there. Fortunately, my apartment-- or our apartment was very close to a-- to the street-- to the corner-- to the wall where they shot people for any crime. If you talked too much, if you walk-- walk bad and all. If you did any kind of crime, you were put to the wall-- [INAUDIBLE] wall. You were shot. So you can imagine, that wall must have-- must have had thousands of bullets, of holes. Even it didn't go through the building, because in Europe, they build the buildings with three or four layers of bricks now for the walls. So it didn't go through. But when we went outside, you now, we saw-- there was a lot of huge, big holes, you know, in the wall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed there all-- my brother, myself, my father stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anybody shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that-- oh, golly. It was like eating the matzo. It was, like, every minute. It was-- one time I saw a man who-- who had worked as a tailor for the Germans, you know. And he forgot to shut-- to turn off the iron. He left the iron on, and they shot-- the Germans, you know, they shot him for that. He was a very prominent man before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would you say were in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was trying to think that before. Probably maybe a couple-- maybe a thousand or a thousand five hundred. It was very-- I didn't-- you know, it was a time when nobody cared for-- for-- for-- for-- how many. Just you were looking for yourself. And you were the only one that you worried about, not anybody else, you know. I-- there was-- this ghetto was-- people who went to work, you know. They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one time these men escaped. One boy, from shooting, escaped. The Germans, the SS, and the Croatians, and the Ukrainians were all over. They went all over looking for this person. I jumped in in somebody's apartment. I saw him. They were running after to catch him, and they had a something that if somebody escaped, they took ten to shoot. And I saw the Germans were running, you now, running after that fellow. He's like about a six- or seven-year-old guy. So I hid myself under somebody's bed. It's funny. This lady still lives in Los Angeles, you know. And every time, she reminds me about it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Germans came in, she was sitting at the table. She was sitting, and they asked her, do you see a fellow jump-- escaping? She said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took the bayonet onto the bed, and they were poking the bayonet, you know. And I saw the bayonet, I put my stomach in. It must have been three inches before it hit me. And they went out in a huff. Said, no Jews here. They walked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Ukraine and the Croatian soldiers arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they are-- they were-- there were Latvians there. There was all different kinds. When they-- when-- mostly-- they were mostly guards for beating Jews, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the prisoners or were they soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were mostly guards. German soldiers, German SS. They were in combining with the German army. Not the regular army, but with the SS or the Gestapo or the SD or the some other or the commanders that were in charge of the concentration camps, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did they behaved? They behaved like animals. They behave like there's nobody else in the world except them. They were in charge of the world. Nobody had power. Nobody had no-- no-- there was nothing to say. Like, they behaved like they wrote the constitution for the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stayed in that ghetto a while until my father had a passport from Buenos Aires. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] on the street. [INAUDIBLE], it's like an order. If you have any kind of passports for any country, like, especially Buenos Aires, America, Brazil, anyplace, please register yourself. You'll be got out-- sent back to the country where your passport is. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father had a passport. Before the war, I think I mentioned that he was in Buenos Aires. But he came back because my mother didn't want to go there because she was afraid to go to Buenos Aires because my grandmother told her not to go. Because she was the only daughter. She didn't want to lose my mother. She told her, don't go there, because the water is very dangerous. She scared  my mother, so my mother didn't go. So my father came back, but he remained with the passport. At the same time, a lady came to our house. I was already-- this was in '43. I was about 17 years old. No, 17 and 1/2. A lady came to our house. She says, Mrs. Steinberg, I want to talk to you personally. I was told later on. Please help my niece. I don't know if she's a niece or a daughter or something. Help me. Put that on that she is the daughter-- the wife of Manny, Mendel. You're going to make a phony marriage certificate. Let's-- I know I cannot be saved. At least save her, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: So someone there made a phony writing certificate, like [INAUDIBLE], you know, in Hebrew, you know. With English-- with the Polish and English, you know. Somebody was very knowledgeable in writing, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he submitted that-- he submitted that-- that piece of paper to the Germans, you know, to the Gestapo. And they took us with also as-- we-- we were considered, like, Argentinian people. So my father was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- so the whole camp was very, very jealous, envious, that we were being taken out, you know, and sent back to-- to Buenos Aires, you know. Meantime, this lady must have been, what, maybe three, maybe five, maybe six years older than me. But she was very skinny, you know. She looked young, you know. But she was an older lady, you know, who, you know, who wanted to marry me in the camp. That camp-- it was a fictitious deal. Just on paper, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father registered. They went up to the kommandantur. He had them registered, you know. He put my name and my brother's name on and her name on them. He were looking when we were going to be taken out, you know, and sent back to Buenos Aires, you know. He said we're going to be-- the Germans said we're going to be-- you're going to go on the Pullman train into the-- maybe to Berlin. From there, a plane or somebody else to go by ship. They're going to take us to Buenos Aires, you know. We really thought that's going to be the most tremendous thing for us in our lifetime. There were people there from-- from America, from Brazil, from-- from South Africa. People, before the war, used to travel. They had some papers, and they showed these papers [INAUDIBLE] hoping that they can be saved, too. Until one day, one time, they took these people out, you know. They say, OK, you come here right now. They gathered up these people, [INAUDIBLE] these people up, you know, to the Gestapo. They didn't take me. They didn't take my brother. They didn't take this girl. They didn't take my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did take your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't take my father. Didn't take none of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They forgot. And they had them all shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why you weren't picked?

speaker: interviewee
text: They forgot about us. Maybe-- what I gather-- maybe-- maybe when my father went to register to the Jewish police something, you know, maybe our name wasn't submitted to the Gestapo or something, you know. But all these people who's-- who-- who were-- who registered [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] were shot. Right-- shot, you know. And none of them remained. I don't know exactly how many there were, but there were quite a few. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from there they-- they made the ghetto much smaller. And they took us in. And from there, we went to a-- to the-- to a ammunition factory to work. They took us out to a regular-- a regular camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom. They completely closed up this camp, this-- this-- this ghetto, the tiny ghetto. They took us in Radom. They had an ammunition factory. It was called-- they made-- used to make the best revolvers for the whole world. The Radom are made from that city, that-- that factory. We-- we arrived there. The first thing they gave us is a number. My number is 26203. They give us-- took away all our clothes. They checked our teeth. If we have diamonds, you know. They shaved our heads. They give us striped uniforms. They give us wooden shoes. No socks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some, who were lucky, they gave maybe an undershirt, a green undershirt. I wasn't lucky enough, because I got a jacket. But I was lucky to live, so I wouldn't worry about them. And we went there, and they give us a triangle to wear. And my father-- my father was not there. Just me and my brother were there. My father remained still working as a tailor in one of these shops there where they-- where they work-- not for the-- not-- different chaps now. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was in that camp. It's very hard. It wasn't easy, you know. Until one time-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was very lucky, you know? Luck sometimes plays a big role in life. They pick six Jews-- Before you tell me this story, we'll stop for a minute. And I want the whole story.
